```rpm
%description
Libarchive is a programming library that can create and read several
different streaming archive formats, including most popular tar
variants and several cpio formats. It can also write shar archives and
read ISO-9660 CDROM images. The bsdtar program is an implementation of
tar(1) that is built on top of libarchive. It started as a test
harness, but has grown and is now the standard system tar for FreeBSD 5
and 6.

This package contains the bsdtar cmdline utility.

%package -n bsdtar
Summary:        Utility to read several different streaming archive formats
Group:          Productivity/Archiving/Compression
Requires:       %{libname} >= %{version}

%description -n bsdtar
This package contains the bsdtar cmdline utility.

%package -n %{libname}
Summary:        Library to work with several different streaming archive formats
Group:          System/Libraries

%description -n %{libname}
Libarchive is a programming library that can create and read several
different streaming archive formats, including most popular tar
variants and several cpio formats. It can also write shar archives and
read ISO-9660 CDROM images. The bsdtar program is an implementation of
tar(1) that is built on top of libarchive. It started as a test
harness, but has grown and is now the standard system tar for FreeBSD 5
and 6.

The libarchive library offers a number of features that make it both
very flexible and very powerful.

- Automatic format detection: libarchive can automatically determine
   both the compression and the archive format, regardless of the
   data source. Most tar implementations do not automatically detect
   the compression format, few implementation that can correctly do
   this when reading from stdin or a socket. (The tar program
   included with Gunnar Ritter's heirloom collection also does full
   automatic format detection.)

- Writes POSIX formats: libarchive writes POSIX-standard formats,
   including "ustar," "pax interchange format," and the POSIX "cpio"
   format.

- Supports pax interchange format: Pax interchange format (which,
   despite the name, is really an extended tar format) eliminates
   almost all limitations of historic tar formats and provides a
   standard method for incorporating vendor-specific extensions.
   libarchive exploits this extension mechanism to support ACLs and
   file flags, for example. (Joerg Schilling's star archiver is
   another open-source tar program that supports pax interchange
   format.)

- Reads popular formats: libarchive can read GNU tar, ustar, pax
   interchange format, cpio, and older tar variants. The internal
   architecture is easily extensible. The only requirement for
   support is that it be possible to read the format without seeking
   in the file. (For example, a format that includes a compressed
   size field before the data cannot be correctly written without
   seeking.)

- High-Level API: the libarchive API makes it fairly simple to build
   an archive from a list of filenames or to extract the entries
   from an archive. However, the API also provides extreme
   flexibility with regards to data sources. For example, there are
   generic hooks that allow you to write an archive to a socket or
   read an archive entry into a memory buffer.

- Extensible. The internal design uses generic interfaces for
compression, archive format detection and decoding, and archive data
I/O. It should be very easy to add new formats, new compression
methods, or new ways of reading/writing archives.

%package -n libarchive-devel
Summary:        Development files for libarchive
Group:          Development/Libraries/C and C++
Requires:       %{libname} = %{version}
Requires:       glibc-devel

%description -n libarchive-devel
Libarchive is a programming library that can create and read several
different streaming archive formats, including most popular tar
variants and several cpio formats. It can also write shar archives and
read ISO-9660 CDROM images. The bsdtar program is an implementation of
tar(1) that is built on top of libarchive. It started as a test
harness, but has grown and is now the standard system tar for FreeBSD 5
and 6.

This package contains the development files.

%package static-devel
Summary:        Static library for libarchive
Group:          Development/Libraries/C and C++

%description static-devel
Static library for libarchive

%prep

%build
%define __builder ninja
%cmake
%cmake_build

%check

%ifarch %arm %ix86 ppc s390

%endif
%ctest $exclude

%install
%cmake_install

find %{buildroot} -type f -name "*.la" -delete -print
rm "%{buildroot}%{_libdir}/libarchive.a"

%post   -n %{libname} -p /sbin/ldconfig
%postun -n %{libname} -p /sbin/ldconfig

%files -n bsdtar
%{_bindir}/bsdcat
%{_bindir}/bsdcpio
%{_bindir}/bsdtar
%{_bindir}/bsdunzip
%{_mandir}/man1/*
%{_mandir}/man5/*

%files -n %{libname}

%{_libdir}/libarchive.so.*

%files -n libarchive-devel

%{_mandir}/man3/*
%{_libdir}/libarchive.so
%{_includedir}/archive*
%{_libdir}/pkgconfig/libarchive.pc

%if %{with static_libs}
%files static-devel
%{_libdir}/%{name}.a
%endif
```

--- Base Skill (OPENSUSE.md) ---
You are an expert openSUSE packager for rpm spec files.

## Spec File Conventions
- %changelog section should not exist in .spec file. You may add new changelog entries into PACKAGE.changes file instead.
- Do NOT use the ?dist macro — rpm supports ~ in version format natively (e.g., 1.0~rc1)
- Use standard SUSE RPM macros: %fdupes, %set_permissions where applicable
- Format spec files to be compatible with spec-cleaner
- Do NOT include empty %clean, %post, %pre, %preun, or %postun sections — omit them entirely
- Have a single dependency per BuildRequires: line
- Do NOT use RPM macros in BuildRequires unless the macro is defined in the same spec file (e.g., %{python_module pytest}) or in the build configuration (_config file in project mode). Macros in BuildRequires that come from external packages are not resolved at build dependency resolution time and will cause unresolvable failures.
- Do NOT remove Requires: lines from spec files unless you have verified in the source code that the package is not needed at runtime
- Always keep the copyright header at the top of spec files — do not remove or alter it
- **Avoid unnecessary changes.** Do not modify lines that have no effect on the build result. Cosmetic changes (whitespace, reordering, rewording comments, reformatting for personal preference) must be omitted. Only make changes that directly fix a build failure, update a version, or implement a user-requested modification.
            
## Build & Packaging Rules
- Use openSUSE Factory (tumbleweed) as default build dist unless a preset exists
- Access git sources via https://src.opensuse.org/pool/ by default
- Avoid osc — it requires OBS credentials; use plain build scripts from package sources instead
- obs-git-init can be used to intialize a new git repository for new packages.
- Never recommend running rpmbuild directly, unless the user is inside the build environment with --deep-analyze (where rpmbuild is available and useful for interactive debugging). If a build failure is caused by a missing package that exists in the project, recommend building that package first via the "build <pkg> first" hint — the tool will automatically build the suggested dependency before retrying the current package.
- The build environment has NO network access. Any code that tries to reach external hosts at build time (e.g., downloading dependencies, fetching resources, running tests that contact remote servers) will fail. Such code must be patched to disable or skip the network-dependent parts, or those tests must be disabled in %check.
- When a _service file exists in a package source directory, run `/usr/lib/build/runservices DIRECTORY` at least once to expand the service into source files before building.
- If the `_service` file uses `obs_scm` source service, replace it by adding `#!RemoteAsset: GIT_URL#TAG` and `#!CreateArchive` as prefix hints on the `Source:` line of the spec file.
- Run `/usr/lib/obs/service/format_spec_file` on each directory that contains a `.spec` file to normalize the spec formatting. Make sure execution of this tool is permitted.
- If a `cd` in the `%prep` section fails, the extraction directory of the tarball doesn't match. Inside the build environment, run `tar -tzf ~/rpmbuild/SOURCES/PACKAGE-VERSION.tar.gz | sed 's|/.*||' | sort -u` to find the actual directory name. Add `-v` to `%autosetup` or `%setup` to make rpm print the extraction directory, then add `-n DIRECTORY` with the correct name to `%autosetup` or `%setup`.
- Comment lines in spec file starting with '#!' are specific hints for or build tooling.
            
## Filesystem & Safety
- Assume Btrfs with Snapper enabled — do not run destructive commands that bypass snapshots
- Respect usrmerge: vendor defaults in /usr/lib or /usr/etc, local admin overrides in /etc
- Use sudo for root actions and always warn the user first
                
## Patch Handling
- Do NOT modify patch files unless you have verified that the patch still applies cleanly. In particular, do not add or remove trailing newlines in patch files — doing so corrupts the patch and breaks the build.
- You must avoid removing functionality from the packaged binaries. Disabling or stripping features in build scripts, configure flags, or %files sections breaks the contract with users who expect the full feature set. Only disable features when explicitly requested or when a dependency is genuinely unavailable and cannot be added.
- Rewrite `%patchX` syntax (e.g., `%patch0`, `%patch1`) into `%patch -PX` syntax (e.g., `%patch -P0`, `%patch -P1`). The old `%patchX` form is deprecated in modern RPM and will cause build failures. The `-PX` flag explicitly selects which patch to apply.
- Ensure that all patches are numbered, no Patch: line, use Patch0: instead.
- A typical patch gets defined with a "Patch42:  fix-crash.patch" line and applied with "%patch -P42 -p1" line.

## Communication
- Be direct, concise, and technical

Name:           python-adb-shell
Version:        0.4.3
Release:        0
Summary:        A Python wrapper for adb shell
License:        Apache-2.0
Group:          Development/Libraries/Python
URL:            https://pypi.org/project/adb-shell
Source:         adb_shell-%{version}.tar.gz
BuildRequires:  fdupes
BuildRequires:  python-rpm-macros
BuildRequires:  %{python_module aiofiles >= 0.4.0}
BuildRequires:  %{python_module cryptography}
BuildRequires:  %{python_module pyasn1}
BuildRequires:  %{python_module rsa}
BuildRequires:  %{python_module setuptools}
BuildArch:      noarch

%description
python adb-shell, it originates from

 https://github.com/google/python-adb/tree/master/adb

%prep
%setup -q -n adb_shell-%{version}

%build
%python_build

%install
%python_install
%python_expand %fdupes %{buildroot}%{$python_sitelib}

# Generate filelists for each Python version (paths relative to BUILDROOT)
for py in 3.11 3.13 3.14; do
  v="${py//./}"
  find ${RPM_BUILD_ROOT}/usr/lib/python${py}/site-packages/adb_shell -type f 2>/dev/null | sed "s|^${RPM_BUILD_ROOT}||" > /tmp/files-adb-shell-py${v}.txt || true
  # Add egg-info files
  for d in ${RPM_BUILD_ROOT}/usr/lib/python${py}/site-packages/adb_shell-*.egg-info; do
    [ -d "$d" ] && find "$d" -type f 2>/dev/null | sed "s|^${RPM_BUILD_ROOT}||" >> /tmp/files-adb-shell-py${v}.txt || true
  done
done

Name:           python-adb-shell
Version:        0.4.3
Release:        0
Summary:        A Python wrapper for adb shell
License:        Apache-2.0
Group:          Development/Libraries/Python
URL:            https://pypi.org/project/adb-shell
Source:         adb_shell-%{version}.tar.gz
BuildRequires:  fdupes
BuildRequires:  python-rpm-macros
BuildRequires:  %{python_module aiofiles >= 0.4.0}
BuildRequires:  %{python_module cryptography}
BuildRequires:  %{python_module pyasn1}
BuildRequires:  %{python_module rsa}
BuildRequires:  %{python_module setuptools}
BuildArch:      noarch

%description
python adb-shell, it originates from

 https://github.com/google/python-adb/tree/master/adb

%prep
%setup -q -n adb_shell-%{version}

%build
%python_build

%install
%python_install
%python_expand %fdupes %{buildroot}%{$python_sitelib}

# Generate explicit filelists per Python version with BUILDROOT-relative paths
mkdir -p /tmp/filelists
cd %{buildroot}

for pyver in 3.11 3.13 3.14; do
    sitelib="usr/lib/python${pyver}/site-packages"
    # Find all adb_shell files
    find "${sitelib}/adb_shell" -type f 2>/dev/null | sed "s|^|/|" > "/tmp/filelists/adbs-py${pyver//./}.txt"
    # Find egg-info dirs and their contents
    for einfo in ${sitelib}/adb_shell-*.egg-info; do
        [ -d "$einfo" ] && find "$einfo" -type f 2>/dev/null | sed "s|^|/|" >> "/tmp/filelists/adbs-py${pyver//./}.txt"
    done
done

+ripgrep (rg) is a line-oriented search tool that recursively searches the current
+directory for a regex pattern. By default, ripgrep respects gitignore rules and
+automatically skips hidden files/directories and binary files. It supports PCRE2
+regular expressions and provides fast searching comparable to grep but much
+quicker on large codebases.
 
 %prep
-%autosetup -n ripgrep-%{version}
+%autosetup -p1
 
 %build
+export CARGO_NET_OFFLINE=1

+install -d %{buildroot}%{_mandir}/man1/
+install -d %{buildroot}%{_datadir}/bash-completion/completions/
+install -d %{buildroot}%{_datadir}/zsh/site-functions/
+target/release/rg --generate-man-page groff > rg.1
+target/release/rg --generate-shell-completion bash > rg.bash
+target/release/rg --generate-shell-completion zsh > rg.zsh
+
 %install
-install -Dm755 target/release/rg %{buildroot}%{_bindir}/rg
+cargo install --root %{buildroot} --path . --offline
+
+# Install generated man page and completions
+install -Dm 644 rg.1 %{buildroot}%{_mandir}/man1/rg.1
+install -Dm 644 rg.bash %{buildroot}%{_datadir}/bash-completion/completions/rg
+install -Dm 644 rg.zsh %{buildroot}%{_datadir}/zsh/site-functions/_rg
+
+# Install documentation and license files
+install -d %{buildroot}%{_docdir}/%{name}
+cp -v README.md COPYING LICENSE-MIT UNLICENSE CHANGELOG.md FAQ.md GUIDE.md \
+   %{buildroot}%{_docdir}/%{name}/
 
 %files
 %{_bindir}/rg
-%license LICENSE-MIT

-%doc README.md

+%{_datadir}/zsh/site-functions/_rg
+%{_mandir}/man1/rg.1.gz
+%{_docdir}/%{name}
+%license COPYING LICENSE-MIT UNLICENSE

[MODIFY] Ollama:
The changes have been applied successfully. Here's a summary of what was added:

1. **New `%package common` subpackage** - Contains the systemd service file for Glances server mode
2. **Systemd service file** (`glances-server.service`) - Created in `%prep`, starts Glances in web server mode (`-w` flag)
3. **Installation in `%install`** - The service file is copied to `%{_unitdir}` (typically `/usr/lib/systemd/system/`)
4. **`%files glances-common` section** - Packages the systemd service file

The systemd service file will be installable separately via `zypper install python-Glances-common` and can be enabled with `systemctl enable --now glances-server.service`.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS ---
### Error Analysis
The build failed during dependency resolution with the error:
```
.: unresolvable (nothing provides insserv)
```
This indicates that the `.spec` file declares a `BuildRequires: insserv`, but this package is unavailable in the openSUSE Tumbleweed repository. `insserv` is a legacy tool for managing SysVinit scripts and has been removed from modern Tumbleweed releases, which rely exclusively on systemd. Keeping this dependency causes the build system to fail before compilation begins.

### Solution
1. **Remove obsolete dependency**: Open the `.spec` file and delete the line containing `BuildRequires: insserv`.
2. **Ensure systemd support**: If the package includes service files or uses systemd macros, ensure you have:
   ```spec
   BuildRequires: systemd
   ```
   instead of `insserv`. Most modern openSUSE packages use `%systemd_post` and related macros which require `systemd`, not `insserv`.

After making these changes, retry the build.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 1) ---
### Error Analysis
The build failed due to a syntax error in the `.spec` file:
```
error: %patchN is obsolete, use %patch N (or %patch -P N): %patch0
```
You are using the deprecated `%patchN` macro syntax (specifically `%patch0`). Modern openSUSE RPM specifications require the explicit `-P` flag to apply patches.

### Solution
Update the `.spec` file to use the correct patch application syntax:

1. **Replace** all instances of `%patchN` with `%patch -PN`.
   - Change `%patch0` to `%patch -P0`.
   - Ensure you specify the strip level explicitly, typically `-p1`.
   
   **Example:**
   ```diff
   - %patch0
   + %patch -P0 -p1
   ```

2. **Verify other patches**: Ensure any other patches defined (e.g., `%patch1`, `%patch2`) are similarly updated to `%patch -P1 -p1`, etc.

3. **Patch definition format**: Confirm your patch headers follow the standard `PatchN:` naming convention rather than `Patch:`, as required by current conventions.

After making these changes, retry the build.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 3) ---
### Error Analysis
The build failed during the dependency resolution phase with the error `broken (can not parse name from sysstat.spec)`. This indicates that the RPM parser could not successfully identify the package name from your `.spec` file.

This failure typically stems from one of the following issues in `sysstat.spec`:
1.  **Missing or Malformed `Name:` Tag**: The `Name:` line is missing, misspelled, or contains invalid characters/macros that prevent resolution.
2.  **Syntax Errors in Header**: There is a syntax error (e.g., unclosed quotes, invalid macro expansion) in the metadata section at the top of the file, causing the parser to abort before identifying the name.
3.  **Removed Copyright Header**: Removing the top-level copyright header or macros defined there might have broken the spec structure or undefined variables required for parsing.

### Solution
1.  **Verify Metadata**: Open `sysstat.spec` and ensure a valid `Name: sysstat` line exists near the top.
2.  **Check Syntax**: Inspect all lines above the `%description` tag for syntax errors, missing colons, or undefined macros (e.g., typos in `%global` definitions).
3.  **Restore Copyright Header**: Ensure the original copyright header at the very top of the file has not been deleted or altered, as required by openSUSE conventions.
4.  **Validate Locally**: If possible, run a syntax check on the spec file locally before rebuilding to catch parsing errors early.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 4) ---
### Error Analysis
The build failed during dependency resolution with the error:
```text
.: unresolvable (nothing provides insserv)
```
This indicates that your `.spec` file declares a build dependency on **`insserv`**, but this package is not available or cannot be resolved in the openSUSE Tumbleweed repository.

`insserv` is an obsolete tool for managing SysVinit scripts and has been removed from modern openSUSE releases, which rely exclusively on `systemd`. Retaining this dependency causes the build system to fail before compilation begins.

### Solution
1.  **Remove Obsolete Dependency**: Open the `.spec` file and delete the line containing `BuildRequires: insserv`.
2.  **Use Systemd if Necessary**: If the package generates or manages service files, ensure you are using modern systemd macros (like `%systemd_post`) and list `BuildRequires: systemd` instead of `insserv`.
3.  **Verify Spec Macros**: Ensure that any initialization-related macros in your spec file have been updated from deprecated SysVinit variants to their systemd equivalents.

After removing the dependency, retry the build.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 6) ---
### Error Analysis
The build failed during dependency resolution with the error: `.: unresolvable (nothing provides fillup-prereq)`.

This indicates that the `.spec` file declares a runtime dependency on **`fillup-prereq`**, but this virtual capability is no longer provided by any package in openSUSE Tumbleweed. Historically, `fillup-prereq` was used to ensure compatibility with scripts using `%fillup_*` macros, but it has been deprecated and removed from modern repositories.

### Solution
You must remove the obsolete dependency from the `.spec` file to allow the build to proceed.

1.  Open the `sysstat.spec` file.
2.  Locate and remove the line:
    ```spec
    Requires: fillup-prereq
    ```
3.  Ensure that you are using standard SUSE macros (e.g., `%fillup_and_set_permissions`) which handle scriptlets correctly without requiring this specific virtual capability. If `fillup` functionality is explicitly needed at runtime, ensure `Requires: fillup` is used instead of the obsolete prereq.

After removing the dependency, retry the build.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 7) ---
### Error Analysis
The build failed during the `%install` phase with a "Operation not permitted" error:
```text
install: cannot change ownership of '.../BUILDROOT/usr/share/man/man8/sa1.8': Operation not permitted
```
This occurs because the `sysstat` Makefiles contain hardcoded flags (`-g root`) in their `install` commands that attempt to set file group ownership to `root`. The build environment runs as a non-root user (`abuild`), which does not have permission to change file ownership or group settings, causing the `install` command to fail.

### Solution
To fix this, you must modify the Makefiles to remove the hardcoded ownership/group flags from the install commands.

1.  **Create a patch** for the relevant Makefile(s) (likely `Makefile.in` or generated `Makefile`) to remove `-g root` (and `-o root` if present) from lines invoking `install`.
2.  **Apply the patch** in your `.spec` file using standard patching procedures (`PatchX:` tag and `%patch` macro).

If manual editing is preferred for a quick fix (though not recommended for reproducible builds):
- Locate lines like `install -m 644 -g root ...`.
- Change them to `install -m 644 ...`.

This allows the files to be installed with the permissions of the build user, which is standard practice in RPM packaging (ownership can be managed via RPM macros or metadata if necessary, but attempting `chown` during a non-root build is incorrect).

The RPM build failed during the `%install` phase with the following error:
```text
install: cannot change ownership of '.../BUILDROOT/usr/share/man/man8/sa1.8': Operation not permitted
```
This indicates that the `make install` command attempted to change file ownership (specifically group ownership to `root`) using flags like `-g root`. The build environment runs as a non-privileged user (`abuild`), which does not have permission to change file ownership or attributes outside of their own scope. Hardcoded ownership changes in Makefiles are incompatible with standard openSUSE build environments that do not run as root.

### Solution
You must modify the build process to prevent `make install` from attempting to change file ownership. Since you cannot simply remove functionality, this is a build environment compatibility fix.

1.  **Create a Patch**: Generate a patch for the relevant Makefiles (likely `Makefile.in` or generated `Makefile`) that removes the `-o root` and `-g root` flags from all `install` commands.
2.  **Apply Patch in Spec**: Add this patch to your `.spec` file using standard patching conventions (`Patch0:` tag and `%patch -P0 -p1`).
    *   Example modification: Change `install -m 644 -g root ...` to `install -m 644 ...`.

**Alternative (if Makefile supports it)**:
If the Makefile defines variables for install commands, you might be able to override them in `%build`, but patching is the most reliable solution for hardcoded flags.

There is no indication of an Out-Of-Memory (OOM) error; the failure is strictly due to permission restrictions during installation.

### Error Analysis
The build failed during the `%install` phase with the error:
```text
install: cannot change ownership of '/home/abuild/rpmbuild/BUILD/sysstat-12.7.9-build/BUILDROOT/usr/share/man/man8/sa1.8': Operation not permitted
```
This occurs because the `make install` command attempts to set file ownership or group permissions (using flags like `-g root`) on files being installed into the build root. The build environment runs as a non-privileged user (`abuild`), which does not have permission to change file ownership (`chown`) or group (`chgrp`) settings, causing the `install` command to fail with "Operation not permitted".

The log indicates that an attempt was made to fix this via a `sed` command in the `%install` section:
```bash
+ sed -i 's/ -o root//g; s/ -g root//g' Makefile
```
However, the build still failed with `-g root` present in the executed command. This implies that the `sed` command did not successfully match and remove all occurrences of the ownership flags in the `Makefile`. This is commonly caused by:
1.  **Tabs or multiple spaces**: The `Makefile` may use tabs (`\t`) instead of spaces for indentation or spacing between arguments (e.g., `644\t-g root`), which the standard space-matching regex (`s/ -g root//g`) misses.
2.  **Variable expansion**: The flags might be defined in a variable (e.g., `INSTALL_FLAGS`) rather than inline, requiring the variable definition to be patched instead of the usage site.

### Solution
To fix this build failure, you must ensure all `-o root` and `-g root` flags are removed from the Makefiles before installation.

1.  **Modify the sed command** in your `.spec` file's `%install` section (or preferably move it to `%prep`/`%build`) to be more robust against tabs and multiple spaces:
    ```bash
    # Replace existing sed command with this version
    sed -i 's/\s\+o root//g; s/\s\+g root//g' Makefile
    ```
    *Note: Ensure you run this command on all relevant files if the build system uses sub-makefiles, though the error specifically points to the main `Makefile` line 504.*

2.  **Verify variable usage**: If the flags are still present after broadening the regex, check if `-g root` is passed via a make variable (like `INSTALL_DATA` or similar). You may need to patch the variable definition instead.

3.  **Alternative Patching**: The most stable long-term solution is to apply a proper diff patch in `%prep` that removes these flags from all Makefiles, ensuring the build environment remains consistent regardless of whitespace variations.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS ---
### Error Analysis
The build failed during the `%install` phase with a **permission error**:
```text
install: cannot change ownership of '/.../BUILDROOT/usr/share/man/man8/sa1.8': Operation not permitted
```
This occurs because the `make install` command attempts to set file ownership (using flags like `-o root -g root`) on files being installed into the build root. The openSUSE build environment runs as a non-privileged user (`abuild`), which does not have permission to change file ownership or group settings (`chown`/`chgrp`).

Your current spec file includes a `sed` command to strip these flags (`sed -i 's/ -o root//g; s/ -g root//g' Makefile`), but it failed to remove all occurrences. This is likely because:
1.  **Sub-directory Makefiles**: The sysstat build system may invoke sub-makes in directories (e.g., `man/`, `nls/`) that have their own Makefiles containing the hardcoded flags, which were not patched by your single-file sed command.
2.  **Regex Matching**: Specific variations in spacing or formatting prevented the regex from matching in some locations.

### Solution
You must ensure *all* instances of `-o root` and `-g root` are removed from the build system before installation. Modify your `%install` section (or preferably `%prep`) to apply this substitution recursively to all `Makefile*` files.

**Implementation:**
Replace your existing targeted `sed` command with a recursive one that processes the entire directory tree:

```spec
# In %install or %prep
find . -name 'Makefile*' -exec sed -i 's/-o root//g; s/-g root//g' {} \;
%make_install DESTDIR=%buildroot
```

This ensures that any hardcoded ownership flags in sub-makes are also stripped, allowing the build to proceed as a non-root user.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS ---
The build failed because the RPM macro `%{suse_update_config}` is not defined in the current build environment.

### Analysis
In the `%build` section of the spec file, the line `%{suse_update_config -f}` was encountered. Because this macro is not defined in the build environment's macro set, `rpmbuild` did not expand it and instead passed the literal string to the shell.

The shell attempted to execute `%{suse_update_config -f}`. The specific error `fg: no job control` is a side effect of how the shell (likely Bash) is interpreting the unexpanded string or a result of a malformed command line in the generated temporary script, where it incorrectly triggered the `fg` (foreground) job control builtin. Since the build runs in a non-interactive shell, `fg` fails with "no job control".

Regardless of the specific shell error, the root cause is the presence of an unexpanded, invalid macro.

### Solution
Remove the line containing `%{suse_update_config -f}` from the `%build` section of the `gawk.spec` file. This macro is not a standard openSUSE Tumbleweed/Factory macro and is not required for the build.

**Action:**
1. Open `gawk.spec`.
2. Locate the `%build` section.
3. Delete the line: `%{suse_update_config -f}`.
4. Save and retry the build.

**Error Analysis:**
The build fails at the macro expansion phase because `gawk.spec` line 44 uses a bare `%patch` macro. Modern RPM (≥4.20) strictly requires explicit patch numbering via the `-P<NUM>` flag. When the parser encounters an unnumbered `%patch`, it cannot resolve which `PatchN:` declaration to target, resulting in the fatal error: `Patch number not specified: patch`.

**Solution:**
Edit `gawk.spec` and locate line 44 in the `%prep` section. Replace the deprecated bare macro with the modern explicit syntax. Assuming this applies the first defined patch with a standard one-level directory strip (typical for gawk):

```spec
# Change this:
%patch
# To this:
%patch -P0 -p1
```

*(Note: If your upstream patches require backup files or different strip levels, preserve those flags after `-p<level>`. Ensure any subsequent patch calls in the spec are similarly numbered sequentially as `%patch -P1 -p1`, `%patch -P2 -p1`, etc.)*

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 2) ---
**Error Analysis:**
The build fails during macro expansion because the spec file invokes patch application commands (e.g., `%patch -P0`) but lacks corresponding `PatchN:` declarations. Modern RPM strictly requires explicit `Patch0:`, `Patch1:`, etc., lines to register source files as patches. Without these definitions, the parser cannot resolve the patch references, resulting in `No patch number X` errors and a failed `%prep` phase.

**Solution:**
Add `PatchN:` declarations to your spec file for every patch you intend to apply. Place these lines immediately after your `SourceN:` block or in a dedicated section:

```spec
# Register patches explicitly
Patch0:  fix-crash.patch
Patch1:  disable-network-tests.patch
Patch2:  update-docs.patch
```

Ensure the filenames match exactly what exists in your `SOURCES/` directory. If a `.diff` or `.patch` file is currently listed under `SourceN:` but needs to be applied, change that line to `PatchN:` (or add a corresponding `PatchN:` line) so RPM treats it as a patch source rather than a regular archive. Recompile after saving the spec file.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 5) ---
**Error Analysis:**
The build fails with `fg: no job control` because `%{suse_update_config}` was enclosed in single quotes (e.g., `'%{suse_update_config} -f'`) within the `%build` section of your spec file. RPM does not expand macros inside single quotes, leaving the literal string passed to bash. This breaks command chaining and triggers a `fg` execution attempt in a non-interactive shell context, causing the fatal exit.

**Solution:**
Remove the single quotes around `%{suse_update_config}` in your spec file's `%build` section so rpm properly expands it. 

Change:
```spec
'%{suse_update_config} -f'
```
To:
```spec
%{suse_update_config -f}
```

Save the spec file and rebuild. Ensure no other macros are inadvertently quoted in macro-expansion contexts.

[FIX MODE] Attempt 6/10 — Diagnosing build failure...
[DIAG] Build log found. Analyzing...

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS ---
**Error Analysis:**
The build fails with `fg: no job control` because rpmbuild disables shell job control (`set +m`) in non-interactive `%build` scripts for security and reproducibility. The macro `%{suse_update_config -f}` invokes a helper (`generic-update-config`/`config_status`) that attempts to use `fg` (foreground a background process). Bash aborts when `fg` is called without job control enabled.

**Solution:**
Remove `%{suse_update_config -f}` from your `%build` section entirely. Modern openSUSE packaging standards rely on `%configure`, which automatically and safely updates `config.status` without triggering shell job control issues. If you have already run `%configure` earlier in `%build`, this line is redundant and can be deleted safely. 

If an explicit update is strictly required after a later stage, wrap it in a subshell that temporarily enables job control:
```spec
+ (set -m; %{suse_update_config -f})
```
However, per openSUSE conventions, removal is the preferred fix. Save the spec and rebuild.

```spec
/usr/share/man/man3/*.gz
```

Insert these lines anywhere within the existing `%files` block. They claim the missing binaries, headers, shell profile hooks, split debuginfo files, dynamically loaded awk modules, and auxiliary documentation without altering existing packaging logic. Save and rebuild.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS ---
**Error Analysis**
The build failed due to a deprecated RPM macro syntax on line 43 of `gawk.spec`. Modern RPM enforces explicit patch selection and rejects bare `%patch` or legacy `%patchN` forms without flags. The parser encountered `%patch` and threw `Patch number not specified: patch`.

**Solution**
1. Open `gawk.spec` and navigate to **line 43**.
2. Replace the invalid macro with modern explicit syntax:
   ```spec
   %patch -P0
   ```
   *(Note: If your spec defines multiple patches (`Patch0:`, `Patch1:`, etc.) and this line is meant to apply a specific one, adjust `-P0` to match the correct index.)*
3. Save the file and restart the build.

**Additional Notes**
- The `WARNING could not set SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH...` message is non-fatal and only impacts reproducible build timestamps. It does not cause the failure.
- Per OpenSUSE packaging guidelines, ensure all remaining legacy `%patchN` calls in the spec are converted to `%patch -PN` format. Running `/usr/lib/obs/service/format_spec_file <directory>` will automatically normalize these macros if you have local access.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 1) ---
**Error Analysis**
The `%build` stage failed due to a malformed macro invocation in `gawk.spec`. The log shows:
```
[    7s] + '%{suse_update_config' '-f}'
```
The closing brace `}` is incorrectly placed inside the trailing single quote, breaking shell quoting. This results in `/var/tmp/rpm-tmp.y210Xr: line 37: fg: no job control`, causing a non-zero exit status and aborting the `%build` section with `Bad exit status`.

**Solution**
1. Open `gawk.spec` and locate the malformed `suse_update_config` call in the `%build` section.
2. Correct the quoting by moving the closing brace outside the quote:
   ```spec
   # INCORRECT (Current)
   '%{suse_update_config' '-f}'

   # CORRECT
   '%{suse_update_config} -f'
   ```
3. Save and restart the build.

**Additional Notes**
- Standard SUSE macro usage requires balanced quotes when passing arguments: `'%{macro_name} arg1 arg2...'`.
- The `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH` warning is non-fatal and does not impact the `%build` failure.
- If other macros in the spec follow this pattern, apply the same correction to avoid further shell parsing errors.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 5) ---
**Error Analysis**
The `%build` stage failed due to a malformed macro invocation in `gawk.spec`. The build log reveals:
```sh
[    7s] + '%{suse_update_config}' -f
```
The single quotes are incorrectly placed, splitting the expanded RPM macro and its argument into separate shell tokens. Bash interprets `'%{suse_update_config}'` as one command and `-f` as an unrelated second command (or invalid syntax), triggering `fg: no job control` (a secondary error from broken shell parsing) and causing a non-zero exit status (`Bad exit status`).

**Solution**
1. Open `gawk.spec` and locate the `%{suse_update_config}` call in the `%build` section.
2. Correct the quote placement by moving the closing quote outside the macro invocation:
   ```spec
   # INCORRECT (Current)
   '%{suse_update_config}' -f

   # CORRECT
   '%{suse_update_config} -f'
   ```
   *(Ensure any following arguments like package names are placed inside the same trailing single quote.)*
3. Save and restart the build.

**Additional Notes**
- When wrapping SUSE RPM macros that require shell escaping in quotes, the pattern must be `'%{macro_name} arg1 arg2...'`. Misaligned braces/quotes cause immediate shell parsing failures during `%build`.
- The `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH` warning is non-fatal and unrelated to this failure.

--- OLLAMA ERROR ANALYSIS (attempt 10) ---
**Error Analysis**
The build failed because several files declared in the `%doc` and `%files` sections do not exist in the extracted source tree or the install destination:
- Missing documentation files: `FUTURES`, `LIMITATIONS`, `PROBLEMS` (referenced in `%doc`).
- Missing binaries: `igawk`, `pgawk` (referenced in `%files`).

RPM strictly validates that all `%files` entries exist at the end of the build. The presence of non-existent paths causes an immediate abort with `error: File not found`. Additionally, the `%doc` section fails when attempting to copy these missing files, propagating the error.

**Solution**
Remove the references to these non-existent files from `gawk.spec` in the `%doc` and `%files` sections to align with the actual contents of the gawk 5.4.0 source tarball.

1. Open `gawk.spec`.
2. In the `%doc` section, change:
   ```spec
   %doc AUTHORS COPYING FUTURES LIMITATIONS NEWS POSIX.STD PROBLEMS
   %doc README README_d
   ```
   to:
   ```spec
   %doc AUTHORS COPYING NEWS POSIX.STD
   %doc README README_d
   ```
3. In the `%files` section, remove:
   ```spec
   /usr/bin/igawk
   /usr/bin/pgawk
   ```
4. Save and restart the build.

**Additional Notes**
- `igawk` and `pgawk` are sometimes conditional on specific configure flags or may have been removed/renamed in newer upstream releases. If they are required at runtime, investigate whether they install to a different path (e.g., `%{_bindir}`) or if additional `BuildRequires:` dependencies are needed to enable their build.
- The `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH` warning is non-fatal and unrelated to this failure.
